items.name: Agent-Skills-for-Context-Engineering
items.runtime_control_rationale: distribution layer looks like discovery / registry; background execution appears supported; local-first posture appears strong; workflow lock-in assessed as low; permission visibility assessed as high
items.execution_boundary: Primarily discovery/index layer; execution usually happens only after the user enters downstream skill repos or tools. Includes delegated/multi-agent coordination, so effective execution may span more than one agent layer.
items.where_code_runs: Mostly local reading/browsing until user installs a downstream skill; actual code execution is deferred to linked tools. MCP support suggests standardized external tool/server handoff is possible.
items.secret_holder: Usually user-held in downstream tools, not in the registry layer itself.
items.audit_evidence: README/docs, repo structure, and linked downstream installation paths provide the main audit evidence. Permission/governance visibility appears relatively strong from available metadata.
items.background_duration_fit: long; repository signals support for unattended or background-style execution.
items.execution_evidence: Registry/readme metadata, linked repos, and installation/docs paths are the main evidence surfaces. Multi-agent/delegation signals suggest checking handoff logs and execution trace visibility.
items.takeover_path: User can usually stop at selection time by not installing or invoking downstream skills; takeover is strongest before execution starts.

items.name: Acontext
items.runtime_control_rationale: distribution layer looks like tooling / runtime; background execution appears supported; local-first posture appears strong; workflow lock-in assessed as medium; permission visibility assessed as high
items.execution_boundary: Acts closer to an execution/runtime layer, so control depends on local setup, invoked tools, and permissions granted by the user.
items.where_code_runs: Typically on the user machine or local runtime/tooling environment, with possible external tool/API handoff.
items.secret_holder: Usually user or local environment variables; may expand to connected tool/API credentials.
items.audit_evidence: CLI commands, config files, schema/validation hints, and explicit protocol/tool integration references. Permission/governance visibility appears relatively strong from available metadata.
items.background_duration_fit: long; repository signals support for unattended or background-style execution.
items.execution_evidence: CLI commands, local config, and repo-documented runtime behavior provide the main evidence surface.
items.takeover_path: User can usually intervene via local CLI, config changes, process stop, or removing tool access from the runtime.

items.name: justdoit
items.runtime_control_rationale: distribution layer looks like skill pack / workflow repo; background execution appears partial; local-first posture appears strong; workflow lock-in assessed as low; permission visibility assessed as medium
items.execution_boundary: Workflow/skill-pack layer; execution boundary depends on the host agent and whichever tools the pack calls into.
items.where_code_runs: Usually inside the host agent environment, often local-first but potentially extended by external tools or protocols.
items.secret_holder: Usually user-held via host agent config, plus any linked APIs/services the workflow requires.
items.audit_evidence: Prompt/workflow files, install instructions, protocol mentions, and governance notes in repo metadata.
items.background_duration_fit: medium; repeatable workflow automation exists, but always-on/background support is only partial.
items.execution_evidence: Protocol references, tool/server docs, and explicit integration points provide the main evidence surface.
items.takeover_path: User should be able to revoke protocol/tool access, stop connected servers, or cut integration at the host-agent boundary.
